speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 18th of September 1995. The survivor is Emanuel Darren. The interviewer is Daniel Feiler. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia, and the interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's September the 18th 1995. It's an interview with Emanuel Darin by myself, Daniel Feiler, in Sydney, Australia, and it's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Emauel Darin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-M-A-N-U-E-L. Darin, D-A-R-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that also?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-N-K-E-L-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 22nd of July, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 81 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a bit about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was from the middle upper class of the Jewry in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They were living in Sosnowiec, which was on the-- in the part of Poland which belonged to Russia. As you know, Poland was divided between Russia, Germany, and Austria. Not far from the German border. It was the industrial part of Poland-- coal mining, steel, et cetera. My father was a merchant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother act like in those days home duties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father did-- was not a very successful merchant. The family generally was quite wealthy, but my father managed to lose the money in the depression, and practically never made good. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My brother was younger than I was. In 1934 he, after matriculating in Poland, he left for Jerusalem, where he started at Hebrew University. His intention was to study agriculture. It was supposed to be opened. And so he-- somebody advised him to study biology so then the agricultural [? stars, ?] those would be counted at the end. Agriculture was not open for the next number of years. And he stayed with the biology, finish the university over at [? tentrus ?] during the study. It was endocrinology, which became a new science then. And in long run it came-- came out as auspices of Bernard [? Sontag ?] and was working with him, and became the assistant professor, or extraordinary professor to [? Sontag ?] who was heading the chair. My brother did it after [? Sontag ?] dies, my brother took over the chair. I will come to my brother's story later. I would like to mention to you one thing, because you might have-- it will be important in my life story. I was little born in Breslau because my mother went over there for confinement. The medicine in Germany was a much higher level than it was in Poland. I was born seven days before the outbreak of the First World War. And unfortunately, I was born with a so-called club foot, which was very bad, so that I couldn't walk at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The orthopedic surgeons in Breslau didn't know how to treat it, so they tried to treat it with a part of a different type of equipment. It was slowly turn it on. I was going every few months with my mother. My mother was taking me to Breslau. And I was not working until I was seven years of age. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It had a bearing on me obviously that leg and foot were a bit smaller, because it didn't develop. And in 1921, when I was seven, we had weekend on the Baltic Sea in Sopot. My mother decided to go to Breslau again for to get new shoes for me. And she went via Berlin during the war. Or coincidentally, my mother met with a German professor of orthopathy-- Professor Gauck-- who then was the president of the German Orthopedic Society. When he saw me in Berlin, he told my mother she is not going anymore to Breslau, that he would take care of speaking to his colleagues about that I need an operation, and if he carries the operation out, I will be walking. This was of close surprise to my mother, who tried everything possible before, and couldn't get the answer to it. So she went to other orthopedic surgeons, because she didn't trust that it is possible. So everyone of them said that if Professor Gauck wants to do it, to let him do it, because he is the top guy. And actually he operated on me. I was for many months in the clinic-- in his clinic. And before the operations my only question was would I be able to play soccer-- or football, as we called it. So when I woke up after the operation, the first thing I ask him if I was promised a football, a-- a ball. And the ball was next to my bed as I still remember. All right. It took a while to, but I started walking. And it never gave me any difficulties later in my life. And I was in the army. I was doing sports. I am a pretty skier. I am still skiing, snow skiing. And I want to say, because it made a difference, influenced quite a bit of my life later. Anyway, coming back to my family, as mentioned, I was going to school, attending a private school-- non-Jewish School. There were some Jews, who was constituted more or less maybe 20% or below of the population. Considering that the cities of Poland, or towns of Poland, had a very high percentage of Jews, this was very little. And I was going to school in Bedzen. This was a place from which my mother came, or her family was living. And Bedzen was another-- it was a Jewish school, one of two only, as far as I know. Schools well teaching Hebrew in Poland, the other one was in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this far from your home in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only 7 kilometers away. And it was connected by tram and by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where in Sosnowiec exactly did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the street was Dembinska, Number 7. It was a pretty modern house, apartment. We all lived in apartments in Europe. People didn't live in houses or villas, . Unless they were connected with some industry and this was outside of town. The house was modern. It was the house-- first house in Poland, first building which had central heating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was still before the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of that area was it? Were there-- was there many Jews living in that area or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all over town were  living Jews. So it was not-- it was not the Jewish part of town. The Jewish part of town was the part, Madreieska, and around where were all the shops. But Jews lived everywhere. Yes. Poland actually had originally two classes-- the land owners and the peasants. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] third class, the town dwellers, this developed originally, it was developed by German colonists and Jews. So it was a problem in the Polish economy. So that really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the merchants were mostly Jews. Poles were not interested in it, and they didn't know how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe your family's religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can say only that I was having Hebrew tuitions. I had bar mitzvah. But I had arrangement with my parents that after bar mitzvah I do what I want. And I did it. I never went back to the synagogue, or temple, unless for special occasion, but not for religious reasons. My father was going, not during the week, or on Saturdays, but on Jewish holidays. He was going there. I attended-- sorry-- I sometimes went with my father on the Jewish holidays just to go with him. I-- generally Poland had 3 and 1/2 millions Jews approximately. The high majority was very religious-- Orthodox religious. I would say 3,400,000. But I was growing completely apart of them, so I can't tell you much about it. I didn't speak Yiddish. I did not have any practical contacts. They were more or less strangers to me. I, you see, I understood maybe some Yiddish because I was bilingual. I spoke German well. I was brought up with German Frauleins, these were, like English nannies or French governesses. And I spoke German as well as Polish. I still speak German well. I leaned French. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So because-- did you have  any Jewish friends or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes at school, school friends. Yes, I had Jewish friends. But I had Polish friends as well. But it was still, I was more with Jewish friends than with Polish friends. These two groups of population, if you can speak this way, Polish and Jews, in private lives, they were living mostly a very separate life. The attitude, Poland was basically a very anti-Semitic country. The Catholic Church did everything possible to put the Poles against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you experience any anti-Semitic incidents in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, towards the end of my school days when I was already in fourth or fifth year, started the anti-Jewish demonstrations in which the schoolboys from [? earlier, ?] from the higher classes, Polish schoolboys took part as well. So I experienced this, yes. But not personality. Nobody ever hit me. Nobody-- but it was a shock to the system.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a boy, what were some of your hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Driving bicycles, riding, swimming. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later on, when I grew up, motor-- motorcars, motorbikes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I didn't have any-- I was not a good tennis player, ever. Skiing later. Skating before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a bit about your family's life at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My mother was a very energetic person. We had-- she, we-- my mother came from a large family group. There were 12 of them. My mother was the youngest. Like in the families, not everybody was good with everybody else, you know. My mother was much younger than her elder part of the family. To those degrees where there are-- that she had nephews and nieces were older than her. And-- but she was very well liked by all of them. She didn't have arguments with any one of them particularly. And so we had quite a bit of entertaining at home. And the members of family were coming as well. And it was another type of entertainment that we have here, because they were mostly dinners. We didn't have this, what we have here, parties or something. This was unknown. This started appearing in Poland only shortly before the Second World War. I was very close particularly with one of my uncles, who was a very accomplished sportsman. Before the First World War, he was taking prizes on bicycles, later in gymkhanas and horses. And after the war, he was a very accomplished motorcar driver. And from him I learned driving. I took as a later on part in trials, country trials with him-- with him driving and me acting as an observer. Otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my cousins, first cousins were all much older  than I was. So I was rather friendly with their children-- with their children, who were older than me on my age. I-- when I came to the-- close to the matriculation, the problem became what to do. I was very pessimistic as far as the future in Poland. And actually I wanted to go and study somewhere out of Poland to learn some practical things like, in my mind, was then dye works, or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fur, dying fur, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] learning to make bricks-- something, a speciality with which I could travel around the world, and did not have borders. At the once you change the border, you couldn't use what you knew. Unfortunately, I couldn't do it, because my parents couldn't afford, in those days already, to pay for my [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was it that you matriculated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I matriculated in 1931. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One, two. And so I decided-- I always was very interested in law, so I decided to study law. And I studied law at the Krakow and the Jagiellonian University, which was oldest university in Poland. I did as well political science.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have any difficulties getting into the University of Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was the law faculty, no. The difficulties were mainly in medical faculty, veterinary science, and engineering. In medical faculty exists the so-called numerous classes. Because Jews formed about 10% of the total population in Poland, 10% of the seats were available for them at the university. So the competition for admission to the university was not against the Poles, or with the Poles. It was one Jew against the other Jew, because there were so many Jewish entries, and it was depending which Jew, who got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in law, you didn't have any

speaker: interviewee
text: In law was no-- at the university level-- was not restriction, because it was no practical restriction how many people attended. They didn't require any special facilities. They had to learn the subject, and pass the exams, take part eventually in seminaries, and this was all. But the restrictions were later. Once you finished at university, the Polish government restricted the possibility for Jews to join the profession by all closing, in some respects, admissions for Jews or by putting them to this type of exams where they couldn't pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you affected by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was affected, because I was already a university graduate before the outbreak of the war in '35, '36. And also-- and I could not-- I knew that I would have difficulties. And I was very pessimistic as far as a future of Jews in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your perspective on-- on the changes that were happening in Germany and the rest of the world at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very pessimistic. I knew-- I expected a war. I have here a correspondence with my brother in Israel, who left in '34. Well, in '36 or so, I was writing to him and telling him that it's-- that it's only a matter of years before the-- a few years before the war will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you expect would happen? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I expect German attack, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you fear something might happen to the Jews-- that Jews would be singled out in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, not to this degree as they were singled out later. [INAUDIBLE] they were killed. But I didn't expect that it would go so far. But I expected that definitely they will suffer greatly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then in this early stage, when you finished your university, what plans did you make for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. It it's much easier to migrate when you see the writing on the suitcases, and has nothing behind you. You don't leave anything behind. Once you are established it's much harder. Somewhere, the mental process is different. And it's much harder to make the decision to leave your parents, to leave your family, to leave everything and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was probably is the reason by all the migrations from Europe be to United States in the beginning of the 20th century, or end of the 19th. Who are migrating? I didn't have any relatives or friends in the United States. Because only the very poor people migrated. The people had nothing to risk, nothing to give up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- later on, the refugees from Poland, they-- they were already a mixed group who migrated, wherever. Somewhere in United States was not my dream country to go, I decided before the outbreak of the war that I would go to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What did you know of Australia at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew two things-- that they are racers in Melbourne, Melbourne Cup, and there is Bank of New South Wales in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that they establish a telegraph line, which reaches London so that they know the prices of wheat on the exchange in London. This was my knowledge. It's like a wild west of the 18th century, or 19th century being in the United States. That's what I knew about. I [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was studying. I was killing the time. I was engaged to a girl, who was one of the richest girls in Poland. But we were interrupted. We never married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you matriculated, did you seek any work? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was working still with a solicitor. But you had to go through it before you could sit for the exam. They were officially not completely closed. It was still some chance that you might go through it. I didn't have much choice. You see, I was-- the family, my family was pretty assimilated-- part of it at least; part maybe was not so much. One of my cousins was, during the first World War head of the Polish underground in Warsaw. My great, great grandfather was treasurer in 1863 of the Polish uprising against the Russians in Radom gubernias, maybe [? it was Kachka ?] gubernia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My great, great, great grandfather somewhere was the chief Rabbi of Warsaw, [? Meisel, ?] who was a member of the national government in the uprising. Look. I had to other relatives who were with Klaus Puszweski, who was the dictator who later became the benevolent dictator of Poland, who later died in the-- during the Second World War, and killed by the Germans in Yugoslavia, because he was in underground somewhere there. He was a medical doctor. He died. It was a hard life, because you knew that you are-- there is not much future for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember of the-- the leading up to-- to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can-- I remember, for example, exactly the nervousness of the immediate prewar days. I remember when in beginning of '39, Poland took-- attacked Czechoslovakia, and took the Teschen region-- region away from Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I was on holidays, and it was 25th or 26th of August. And after the lunch, I was playing cards-- bridge-- with friends in the garden. And came the announcement that Ribbentrop was in Berlin, and signs the nonaggression pact with the Russians. I interrupted the play and said this is war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And This? Was actually practically mentally for me the start of the Second World War. I immediately, the next day we left. I-- we-- I put my parents on a train directly to Warsaw, because we were very close to German border. I went still to Sosnowiec to take some things from the apartment, what we could, and joined them in Warsaw. My parents stayed there with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with my father's sister, who in the meantime escaped with her family, Warsaw going east. She stayed in their apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an interesting story, because I had with me two dogs-- Airedales. One of the dogs was mine, and one belonged to my uncle. And this was a gift from my uncle. So I took them to-- with me to Warsaw. I know one of the dogs, I took her to the other part of Warsaw where my parents lived. And then I brought him back. What came out after I escaped Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the dog, this part where my girlfriend was living was to become very bombarded heavily. And the dog escaped two days earlier before the bombardment, and found to one part of Warsaw to the other part of Warsaw the way to my parents, but he was only once on the third floor of our apartment building. And he slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just take a break here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. Yes, I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Emanuel Daren. It's the 18th of September, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
